explanation: Answer: C | The volute casing is a spiral-shaped passage that surrounds the impeller. As fluid exits the impeller at high velocity, the gradually expanding cross-section of the volute slows the fluid (velocity decreases), converting kinetic energy to pressure energy (Bernoulli's principle). The volute also collects and directs flow to the discharge nozzle.

Answer: C | Centrifugal pumps are classified as dynamic (kinetic) machines because they add energy to the fluid continuously through centrifugal force and impeller rotation, rather than trapping and compressing a fixed volume of fluid. Flow rate varies with system pressure. Positive displacement pumps (gear, piston, diaphragm) trap and displace fixed volumes per stroke or revolution.

question_text: When installing a horizontal roller chain drive, the tight (driving) side should be:
option_a: Either side depending on drive direction
explanation: Answer: C | For horizontal chain drives, the tight (driving) side should be on top. This allows the slack (loose) side on the bottom to sag naturally without creating excessive chain whip. The tensioner or idler, if used, is applied to the slack (bottom) side. This is standard per ANSI/ASME B29.1 chain drive installation guidelines.

Answer: B | Quenching is the rapid cooling of steel from above its critical temperature (typically 800-900°C for medium carbon steel) in water, oil, or polymer quench media. The rapid cooling traps carbon in a distorted crystal structure called martensite, which is very hard and brittle. Quenching is followed by tempering to reduce brittleness while retaining hardness.

question_text: When using cross dial method, sag must be:
explanation: Answer: D | Indicator bracket sag must be eliminated or compensated for in all dial indicator alignment methods. Gravity causes the bracket and indicator to deflect downward at the bottom position, giving a false reading. Sag is measured by supporting both ends of the bracket and noting the indicator change between top and bottom positions. This sag value is added to bottom readings in calculations.

Answer: D | Bearing internal clearance class C3 indicates greater than normal (CN) internal clearance between the rolling elements and races. C3 is specified when the bearing will experience tight interference fits, elevated operating temperatures, or both  conditions that reduce internal clearance. C2 = less than normal; CN = normal; C3 and C4 = greater than normal.

Answer: C | A Woodruff key has a semicircular (half-moon) shape. It sits in a circular keyway milled in the shaft and fits into a rectangular keyway in the hub. Woodruff keys are self-aligning and easy to install, making them common in automotive and light machinery applications. They are sized by a number system (e.g., #808 = 1/4 wide x 1 diameter).

Answer: D | An Oldham coupling compensates for parallel (lateral) offset misalignment using a three-piece design: two flanged hubs with tongue projections at 90° to each other and a central disk with mating slots. The central disk slides laterally in both directions to accommodate offset while transmitting torque. It cannot compensate for angular misalignment.

question_text: The main cause of premature V-belt failure is:
explanation: Answer: D | Misalignment and improper tension are the leading causes of premature V-belt failure. Misalignment causes uneven wear on belt sidewalls and generates heat. Over-tensioning overloads bearings and stretches belts; under-tensioning causes slippage, glazing, and heat buildup. Both conditions dramatically reduce belt life.

question_text: The main advantage of mechanical seals over packing is:
explanation: Answer: C | Mechanical seals provide virtually zero leakage (typically just a thin film for lubrication of the seal faces, often invisible as vapor). Compression packing always requires a controlled drip rate for lubrication. Mechanical seals also reduce shaft sleeve wear, lower power consumption, and are required for pumping hazardous or toxic fluids where leakage cannot be tolerated.

option_d: Carries combined radial and thrust loads
explanation: Answer: D | Tapered roller bearings carry combined radial and axial (thrust) loads simultaneously. The tapered geometry of the rollers and raceways generates an axial component of force, so they must be mounted in opposing pairs (face-to-face or back-to-back) to handle thrust in both directions. They are adjustable by varying the preload between the opposing bearing pair.

Answer: D | Arc blow is caused by magnetic fields in the workpiece or circuit that deflect the arc from its intended path. It occurs mainly with DC welding on ferromagnetic materials near the end of joints, at changes in section, or when using high amperage. Remedies include switching to AC, changing work lead position, welding toward the ground connection, or using a back-step technique.

question_text: The purpose of a tensioner in a chain drive is to:
explanation: Answer: C | A tensioner in a chain drive maintains proper chain tension as the chain wears and elongates over time. Proper tension prevents excessive sag on the slack side (which causes chain whipping and jump-off) while avoiding over-tensioning (which accelerates wear on pins, bushings, and sprocket teeth). Spring-loaded or adjustable idler sprockets are commonly used.

Answer: D | NLGI (National Lubricating Grease Institute) grades classify grease consistency/stiffness from 000 (very soft, semi-fluid) to 6 (very hard, block grease). NLGI 2 is the most common industrial grade  firm enough to stay in place but soft enough to flow to bearing contact areas. The thickener type (lithium, calcium, polyurea) also affects performance.

option_a: Reduces hydraulic closing force on faces
explanation: Answer: A | A balanced mechanical seal is designed with a step in the shaft or sleeve so that the hydraulic closing force acting on the seal face is reduced. The balance ratio (typically 0.7-0.8) limits the closing force to prevent excessive face wear and heat generation at high pressures. Balanced seals are used for pressures above approximately 100-150 psi.

Answer: B | Bearing seals (contact seals) and shields (non-contact) serve a dual purpose: they prevent contaminants (dirt, water, metal particles) from entering the bearing, and they retain the lubricant (grease or oil) inside the bearing. Contact seals provide better exclusion but generate more friction and heat. Contamination and lubricant loss are the two leading causes of premature bearing failure.

question_text: A pump operating off its curve:
option_b: Can cause reduced life and efficiency
explanation: Answer: B | A centrifugal pump operating far from its BEP (off its curve) suffers increased radial thrust loading on the shaft and bearings, internal recirculation at low flows or recirculation/cavitation at high flows, elevated vibration, increased power consumption or cavitation damage, and accelerated seal and bearing wear. Prolonged off-BEP operation significantly reduces pump life.

question_text: In Canada, oxygen cylinders used for oxy-fuel cutting and welding are typically:
explanation: Answer: D | In Canada, oxygen cylinders are painted black per CSA/CGA standards. This differs from some other countries. Acetylene cylinders are typically red in Canada. Color coding is a secondary identification method only  always read the cylinder label and valve connection type as the primary identification. Never rely on color alone.

question_text: The inner race of a bearing is typically mounted:
explanation: Answer: D | The inner race (ring) is mounted with an interference (press) fit on the rotating shaft so it rotates with the shaft without creeping. The outer race is mounted with a clearance or slight interference fit in the housing, depending on whether the outer ring rotates or is stationary. Creep (spinning) of either ring causes rapid wear and heat generation.

question_text: The basic dynamic load rating (C) of a bearing:
option_b: Is the load for 1 million revolutions with 90% reliability
explanation: Answer: B | The basic dynamic load rating (C) is the constant load under which a group of identical bearings will achieve a basic rating life (L10) of 1 million revolutions with 90% reliability (10% failure rate). It is used in bearing life calculations: L10 = (C/P)^p x 10^6 revolutions, where P = equivalent dynamic load and p = 3 for ball bearings, 10/3 for roller bearings.

question_text: Acetylene cylinder pressure should not exceed:
explanation: Answer: A | Acetylene working pressure must never exceed 15 psi (103 kPa). Above this pressure, acetylene becomes unstable and can decompose explosively, independent of the oxygen supply. Acetylene cylinders contain the gas dissolved in acetone on a porous filler material  never lay acetylene cylinders on their side as this allows liquid acetone to flow into the regulator.

Answer: A | Multistage centrifugal pumps have multiple impellers arranged in series on a common shaft, with the discharge of each stage feeding the inlet of the next. Each stage adds head (pressure), so the total head is the sum of all stages. Multistage pumps are used where high pressure is required (boiler feedwater, high-pressure water injection, pipeline pumps).

option_d: The shaft from wear and corrosion
explanation: Answer: D | Shaft sleeves are replaceable cylindrical sleeves pressed or threaded onto the shaft in the stuffing box area to protect the shaft from wear and corrosion caused by packing friction, process fluid, and flush liquid. When worn, sleeves are replaced rather than the entire shaft, significantly reducing maintenance cost. They are typically made from hardened stainless steel or coated alloys.

Answer: D | A gear coupling uses internal and external gear teeth to transmit torque and accommodate angular misalignment (typically up to 1.5 degrees). The tooth profile allows slight angular offset between hubs. Gear couplings require periodic lubrication (grease or oil) and are used in heavy-duty, high-torque applications.

question_text: The cross dial (reverse dial indicator) alignment method uses:
option_b: One dial indicator spanning the coupling gap
option_d: Two dial indicators, one mounted on each shaft or coupling half, facing each other
explanation: Answer: D | The cross dial (reverse dial indicator) method uses two dial indicators, each mounted on one shaft or coupling half and reading on the other shaft. Both shafts are rotated together 360 degrees. This method measures both angular and parallel misalignment simultaneously and eliminates bracket sag errors more easily than rim-and-face methods.

Answer: A | Cylindrical roller bearings carry very heavy radial loads due to the line contact between the cylindrical rollers and the flat raceways  far greater contact area than ball bearings. Most types (NU, NJ, N) carry little or no axial (thrust) load. They are used in gearboxes, electric motors, and rolling mills where high radial capacity and rigidity are needed.

question_text: The main advantage of the cross dial (reverse dial) alignment method over the rim-and-face method is:
option_c: It is faster to set up
option_d: It is less affected by axial shaft movement and provides more accurate angular measurement
explanation: Answer: D | The cross dial (reverse dial) method is less sensitive to axial shaft float than rim-and-face because both indicators measure radially. Axial movement affects both indicators equally and cancels out in calculations. It also gives a longer effective measurement span for angular misalignment, improving accuracy. Both methods require rotating shafts together.

Answer: B | Open impellers (no shroud on either side) are used for pumping liquids containing suspended solids, slurries, and fibrous materials because the open design prevents clogging. They are less efficient than closed impellers due to increased internal leakage but are easier to clean and adjust the running clearance without disassembly.

Answer: B | ABEC (Annular Bearing Engineers Committee) ratings define bearing precision/tolerance classes. ABEC 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 are standard grades  higher numbers indicate tighter tolerances on bore, OD, width, and runout. ABEC 1 is standard industrial grade; ABEC 7 and 9 are used in precision machine tools and high-speed applications.

Answer: D | An interference fit (also called a press fit or force fit) means the shaft diameter is larger than the hole diameter. Assembly requires pressing or heating and creates a compressive stress that locks the parts together without additional fasteners. The amount of interference (0.001 to 0.003 per inch of diameter is typical) determines the holding force.

Answer: A | Compression packing requires a controlled drip rate (typically 40-60 drops per minute or more, per manufacturer guidelines) for lubrication and cooling of the shaft sleeve. A completely dry stuffing box causes excessive heat, scoring of the shaft sleeve, and rapid packing failure. Adjust gland follower gradually to achieve the correct leak rate without over-tightening.

question_text: Before disassembling a pump, you should:
explanation: Answer: A | Before disassembling a pump, mark the positions of the coupling halves, shaft, and all major components to ensure correct reassembly orientation. Also: isolate and lock out the energy source, drain the pump, flush if handling hazardous fluids, take vibration/temperature readings for baseline comparison, and photograph the assembly.

Answer: D | NPSH = Net Positive Suction Head. NPSHa (available) is the suction energy available at the pump inlet; NPSHr (required) is the minimum suction energy the pump needs to operate without cavitating. NPSHa must always exceed NPSHr by a safety margin (typically 1.5-2 m). NPSH is measured in feet or meters of head.

question_text: Excessive pump vibration can be caused by:
explanation: Answer: C | All three causes  misalignment, cavitation, and worn bearings  produce excessive pump vibration. Misalignment generates cyclic radial forces on bearings; cavitation causes violent implosive shock loads on the impeller and casing; worn or failed bearings have increased internal clearance that allows shaft whip. Each must be investigated when diagnosing vibration.

question_text: The most common type of rolling element bearing is:
explanation: Answer: D | Deep groove ball bearings are the most common type of rolling element bearing due to their versatility, low friction, ability to carry both radial and moderate thrust loads, availability in a wide range of sizes, and relatively low cost. They are used in motors, pumps, gearboxes, and virtually all rotating machinery.

option_d: Consistent processes and standards are met
explanation: Answer: D | A quality management system (QMS) such as ISO 9001 establishes documented processes and standards to ensure products and services consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements. It includes procedures for process control, inspection, nonconformance management, corrective action, and continuous improvement. QMS reduces errors, improves consistency, and provides traceability.

question_text: When pressing a bearing onto a shaft, force should be applied to:
explanation: Answer: C | When pressing a bearing onto a shaft, force must be applied only to the inner race  never through the rolling elements or cage. Applying force to the outer race would transmit it through the rolling elements, indenting (brinelling) the races and causing immediate bearing damage. Use a proper bearing press sleeve that contacts only the inner race.

question_text: Bearing failure due to contamination shows:
explanation: Answer: C | Contamination by hard particles (dirt, metal chips) causes false brinelling and indentations (denting) on the bearing races and rolling elements. These indentations create stress concentrations that initiate fatigue spalling. Contamination is the leading cause of premature bearing failure. Proper sealing, clean installation practices, and clean lubricants are essential.

question_text: Pump curves show the relationship between:
explanation: Answer: B | Pump performance curves plot head (pressure) versus flow rate, with additional curves showing pump efficiency, power consumption (BHP), and NPSH required  all as functions of flow. The intersection of the pump curve and the system resistance curve defines the operating point. These curves are provided by pump manufacturers for each impeller diameter and speed.

Answer: A | Bearing preload removes internal clearance by applying a compressive load between the rolling elements and races. This eliminates play, increases stiffness, improves running accuracy, and reduces noise. Preload is used in pairs of angular contact or tapered roller bearings. Excessive preload causes overheating and early failure; insufficient preload allows vibration and reduced accuracy.

option_c: Is a housed bearing unit for shaft support
explanation: Answer: C | A pillow block (plummer block) is a self-contained, housed bearing unit consisting of a bearing inserted into a cast iron or steel housing with a flat base for mounting. It supports a shaft externally and is bolted to a frame or structure. The housing protects the bearing and typically includes a lubrication fitting. Pillow blocks simplify installation and allow shaft height adjustment by shimming.

question_text: When installing a mechanical seal, cleanliness is:
explanation: Answer: C | Cleanliness is critical when installing mechanical seals because even microscopic particles on the polished seal faces can cause scratches that lead to leakage. Seal faces (typically carbon/ceramic or tungsten carbide/silicon carbide pairs) are lapped to within 3 helium light bands of flatness. Handle faces with lint-free gloves; clean with isopropyl alcohol.

question_text: When lighting an oxy-fuel torch, you should:
option_d: Open acetylene first, ignite, then add oxygen
explanation: Answer: D | Proper oxy-fuel torch lighting procedure: (1) Open acetylene valve slightly (1/4 to 1/2 turn), (2) ignite with a friction lighter (never matches or open flame), (3) adjust acetylene until black smoke disappears, (4) gradually open oxygen valve to achieve the correct flame type (neutral, carburizing, or oxidizing) for the application. Never use a lighter that requires thumb pressure near an open flame.

Answer: A | In a mechanical seal, the rotating seal face (also called the primary ring) is attached to and rotates with the shaft. It runs against the stationary seat (mating ring) which is fixed in the seal gland. The lapped, flat mating faces (typically carbon vs. ceramic, or tungsten carbide vs. silicon carbide) create the primary leak barrier. The extremely thin fluid film between faces provides lubrication.

Answer: B | Rigid couplings (sleeve, clamp, or flanged types) transmit torque without any flexibility and require near-perfect shaft alignment  typically within 0.001 to 0.002 TIR. Any misalignment is transmitted directly as bending loads to the shafts and bearings. Rigid couplings are used where precise shaft positioning is required, such as multi-bearing shaft systems.

Answer: D | Compression packing rings must be installed with staggered joints (each ring rotated 90° or 120° from the previous one) to prevent a straight leak path through the joints. Each ring should be seated individually before adding the next. Never install all rings at once. Use the correct packing size and material for the application.

Answer: C | Torque = Force x radius (moment arm). It is a rotational force measured in ft-lb or N-m. Example: a 50 lb force applied at a 2-foot radius produces 100 ft-lb of torque. In machinery, torque is what does the work of driving loads, while power (HP) describes how quickly that torque is delivered.

option_a: Filling the pump with liquid before starting
explanation: Answer: A | Priming fills the pump casing and suction line with liquid before startup. Centrifugal pumps cannot pump air  they rely on liquid to create centrifugal action and generate head. Without priming, the pump spins but cannot develop suction or discharge pressure, and the mechanical seal or packing may run dry and be damaged.

question_text: Shaft runout is measured with a:
explanation: Answer: B | Shaft runout (eccentricity or wobble) is measured with a dial indicator. The indicator tip contacts the shaft surface while the shaft is rotated; the total indicator reading (TIR) is the runout value. Acceptable runout varies by application: precision shafts typically < 0.001 TIR; general industrial < 0.002 TIR.

option_a: Both rotate at the same speed
option_b: The driver is twice as large
option_c: The driver rotates twice for each rotation of the driven
option_d: The driven rotates twice for each rotation of the driver
explanation: Answer: C | A gear ratio of 2:1 (driver:driven) means the driver gear rotates twice for every one rotation of the driven gear. The driven gear turns at half the speed but produces twice the torque. Speed ratio = driver teeth / driven teeth = driven RPM / driver RPM.

option_b: Type of metal by spark pattern
explanation: Answer: B | A spark test identifies the approximate type and carbon content of steel by grinding a sample on a bench grinder and observing the spark pattern. High-carbon steel produces bright, bushy sparks with many star bursts (explosions). Wrought iron produces long, straight orange lines. Cast iron produces short, dull red sparks. Stainless steel produces long, wavy orange sparks. It is a field identification method, not a precise test.

question_text: The taper on a tapered key is typically:
explanation: Answer: B | Standard tapered keys have a taper of 1/8 inch per foot (1:96 taper) per ASME B17.1 standards. This taper allows the key to wedge tightly in the keyway when driven in, providing both torque transmission and axial retention. Gib head keys use the same taper but have a head for easier removal.

Answer: A | A flexible coupling transmits torque while accommodating minor shaft misalignment (angular, parallel offset, and axial) through a flexible element such as rubber, elastomer, grid, or disc. It does not substitute for proper alignment but absorbs small residual misalignment and dampens vibration and shock loads.

option_a: From outside edge to outside edge
option_c: Along the outside of the chain
option_d: From center to center of adjacent pins
explanation: Answer: D | Chain pitch is the distance from the center of one pin to the center of the adjacent pin. It is the fundamental measurement for chain classification per ANSI/ASME B29.1 standards. Common pitches are 3/8, 1/2, 5/8, 3/4, and 1. Sprockets must match the chain pitch exactly.

Answer: A | SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding), commonly called stick welding, uses a consumable flux-coated electrode. The flux coating burns to create a shielding gas that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination, and forms slag that covers and protects the solidifying weld. SMAW is versatile, portable, and effective outdoors but slower than wire-feed processes.

question_text: Welding electrode E7018 has a tensile strength of:
explanation: Answer: C | Electrode classification E7018: E = electrode, 70 = 70,000 psi minimum tensile strength, 1 = all-position welding, 8 = low-hydrogen iron powder coating, DCEP or AC current. The 18 indicates a low-hydrogen electrode requiring storage in a rod oven at 250-300°F (120-150°C) to prevent moisture absorption and hydrogen-induced cracking.

question_text: The tolerance for alignment depends on:
option_a: Speed, coupling type, and manufacturer specs
explanation: Answer: A | Shaft alignment tolerances depend primarily on operating speed (RPM), coupling type, and manufacturer specifications. Higher RPM requires tighter tolerances. For example, at 1800 RPM: typical acceptable angular misalignment is 0.002 per inch of coupling diameter; parallel offset < 0.003. At 3600 RPM, tolerances are roughly half these values. Always follow specific coupling manufacturer limits.

question_text: A flag symbol on a weld indicates:
explanation: Answer: A | A flag symbol (a filled triangular flag at the junction of the reference line and arrow) on a welding symbol indicates a field weld  a weld to be made at the installation site rather than in the fabrication shop. This distinguishes site welds from shop welds on assembly drawings, which is important for quality control, inspection scheduling, and cost tracking.

question_text: The induction heating method for bearing installation:
explanation: Answer: D | Induction heating uses an electromagnetic field to heat the bearing inner race rapidly and uniformly, causing it to expand thermally so it slides onto the shaft without hammering. Heat to 80-100°C maximum (never exceed 120°C, which can alter the steel's hardness). Use a demagnetizer after heating. This method prevents brinelling damage from impact installation.

question_text: A check valve in a pump system:
explanation: Answer: B | A check valve (non-return valve) in a pump discharge system allows flow in one direction only, preventing reverse flow (backflow) when the pump stops. Without a check valve, the liquid column in the discharge piping would flow backward through the pump, potentially rotating it in reverse, water hammering, and draining the suction line, causing repriming issues.

question_text: The impeller in a centrifugal pump:
option_c: Rotates and imparts energy to the fluid
explanation: Answer: C | The impeller is the rotating component that imparts energy to the fluid. As it rotates, centrifugal force accelerates fluid outward from the eye (center) to the periphery, converting shaft power into kinetic energy (velocity). The volute casing then converts this velocity into pressure energy. Impeller design (diameter, vane angle, number of vanes) determines pump performance.

Answer: B | The cross dial (reverse dial) method detects both angular misalignment and parallel offset misalignment simultaneously. Angular misalignment is identified by readings that change in opposite directions (one indicator increases while the other decreases). Parallel offset is identified by readings that change in the same direction. The graphical or mathematical method separates the two components.

Answer: A | Pump surge (also called hydraulic surge or water hammer) is characterized by unstable, fluctuating flow and pressure  the pump repeatedly operates at different points on its curve. Common causes include operating far from BEP, valve hunting, air entrainment, or a system curve that intersects the pump curve at an unstable point. Surge causes vibration, noise, and accelerated wear.

option_b: A measure of fluid's resistance to flow
explanation: Answer: B | Viscosity is a fluid's resistance to flow (internal friction). Higher viscosity oils are thicker and flow more slowly; lower viscosity oils are thinner. ISO VG (Viscosity Grade) numbers refer to kinematic viscosity in centistokes (cSt) at 40°C. Selecting the correct viscosity for operating temperature and speed is critical for adequate lubrication film thickness.

Answer: A | The stuffing box (also called the seal chamber) is the cavity in the pump casing where the shaft exits the pressure boundary. It houses either compression packing rings or a mechanical seal to prevent process fluid from leaking along the shaft to atmosphere. The stuffing box gland (follower) compresses the packing or retains the mechanical seal.

question_text: Proper belt tension can be checked by:
explanation: Answer: A | Proper belt tension is verified by measuring deflection under a specified force at the midpoint of the longest span. The manufacturer specifies the acceptable deflection range (typically 1/64 per inch of span). Too much deflection indicates under-tension (slippage); too little indicates over-tension (premature bearing failure).

question_text: Wear rings in a centrifugal pump:
explanation: Answer: C | Wear rings (also called wearing rings or case rings) create a close clearance labyrinth between the rotating impeller and stationary casing to minimize internal recirculation of high-pressure fluid back to the suction side. They are made of a softer material than the impeller so they can be replaced when worn, restoring pump efficiency.

Answer: B | V-belts are identified by their cross-sectional profile (A, B, C, D, E for classical; 3V, 5V, 8V for wedge belts) which determines the top width and depth. The belt number also includes the length (e.g., B75 = B-section, 75 inches outside circumference). The cross-section must match the sheave groove profile for proper wedging action and power transmission.

question_text: In cross dial alignment, if both dial readings increase in the same direction:
explanation: Answer: B | In cross dial alignment, when both indicators show readings that change in the same direction (both increase or both decrease) as the shafts rotate, parallel (offset) misalignment is present. With angular misalignment only, readings would change in opposite directions. Analyzing the top/bottom and left/right readings separately identifies the type and magnitude of misalignment.

question_text: In cross dial alignment, readings are taken at:
explanation: Answer: B | Cross dial alignment readings are taken at four clock positions: 12 o'clock (top), 3 o'clock, 6 o'clock (bottom), and 9 o'clock  representing 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° of shaft rotation. The top and bottom readings determine vertical misalignment; the side readings determine horizontal misalignment. Both angular and offset components are calculated from these four readings.

option_c: Prevents flame from traveling back into hoses
explanation: Answer: C | Flashback arrestors are safety devices installed on both the torch inlets and/or regulator outlets that contain a sintered metal filter and check valve. If a flashback (flame traveling back into the hose) occurs due to a blocked tip or reverse flow, the arrestor's thermal cutoff and check valve extinguish the flame and prevent it from reaching the cylinders.

question_text: Bearing operating temperature should be monitored because:
explanation: Answer: D | Excessive bearing temperature (above 80-90°C for most grease-lubricated bearings) indicates problems such as over-lubrication, under-lubrication, contamination, overloading, misalignment, or insufficient clearance. Monitor with infrared thermometer or thermocouple. Rule of thumb: if bearing is too hot to hold (>60°C), investigate the cause.

Answer: A | O-rings in mechanical seals provide static sealing at secondary seal points  between the rotating face and shaft (or sleeve), and between the stationary face and gland plate. They do not rotate (or rotate very slowly during axial movement) and prevent leakage along the shaft and between seal components. O-ring material is selected for chemical compatibility with the process fluid.

Answer: B | Gearing trades speed for torque (or vice versa)  this is mechanical advantage. In a speed reduction (large driven gear), output speed decreases but output torque increases proportionally. In a speed increase (small driven gear), output speed increases but output torque decreases. Power remains constant (minus losses).

Answer: B | Graphical alignment methods (plotting dial indicator readings on graph paper scaled to represent the machinery dimensions) help the technician visualize where the machine feet need to be shimmed and by how much to correct both angular and parallel misalignment in both vertical and horizontal planes simultaneously. This reduces the number of alignment iterations needed.

Answer: A | Non-destructive testing (NDT) methods include: radiography (X-ray/gamma ray), ultrasonic testing (UT), magnetic particle testing (MT), liquid penetrant testing (PT/dye penetrant), eddy current testing, and visual inspection. These methods detect internal and surface flaws without damaging the part. Destructive tests (tensile, impact, hardness) require test specimens that are consumed in the test.

question_text: The arrow side of a welding symbol is:
option_d: The side the arrow points to
explanation: Answer: D | The arrow side of a welding symbol indicates the joint side that the arrow physically points to. Weld information placed below the reference line applies to the arrow side; information above the line applies to the other side (far side). When the arrow points to the side where the weld is to be made, the weld symbol is placed below the reference line.

option_c: Relative rotation between shaft and hub
explanation: Answer: C | A key is installed in matching keyways cut in both the shaft and hub to prevent relative rotation between them while transmitting torque. Keys are designed as shear elements and are sized to handle the torque load. They do not prevent axial (end-to-end) movement unless a setscrew or retaining ring is also used.

question_text: Horsepower (HP) is calculated using torque and speed as:
explanation: Answer: C | The formula HP = (Torque in ft-lb x RPM) / 5252 is derived from the definition of horsepower (33,000 ft-lb/min) and the conversion factor for rotational work. The constant 5252 = 33,000 / (2 x pi). Example: 100 ft-lb of torque at 1000 RPM = (100 x 1000) / 5252 = 19.04 HP.

question_text: Set screw holding power depends on:
option_a: Thread size and cup point contact
explanation: Answer: A | Set screw holding power depends primarily on thread size (which determines the tightening torque and clamping force) and the point type. A cup point digs into the shaft surface to provide mechanical interlocking in addition to friction. Flat and oval points rely on friction only. Cone points are used in drilled indentations for the highest holding power.

option_c: Causes damage from vapor bubble collapse
option_d: Only occurs in positive displacement pumps
explanation: Answer: C | Cavitation occurs when local pressure in the pump drops below the fluid's vapor pressure, forming vapor bubbles. When these bubbles move to higher pressure regions, they implode violently, causing pitting and erosion of the impeller and casing. Signs include crackling/rattling noise, vibration, reduced flow and head, and visible impeller damage.

Answer: C | Standard gear tooth nomenclature includes: pitch circle (theoretical circle where gears mesh), addendum (tooth height above pitch circle), dedendum (tooth depth below pitch circle), whole depth, face width, diametral pitch, and pressure angle. These terms are defined in AGMA and ISO gear standards.

question_text: A pump's best efficiency point (BEP) is:
explanation: Answer: D | The Best Efficiency Point (BEP) is the flow rate at which the pump operates at maximum hydraulic efficiency  where the least energy is wasted as heat, vibration, and recirculation. Operating near BEP minimizes radial thrust on the shaft and bearings, reduces vibration, and maximizes pump life. Pump selection should target BEP at or near the expected operating flow.

question_text: The purpose of a coupling is to:
explanation: Answer: B | A coupling connects two shafts end-to-end to transmit rotational power (torque) from a driver (motor, engine) to a driven machine (pump, gearbox, compressor). Couplings also isolate vibration and shock, accommodate thermal expansion, and allow disconnection for maintenance. The coupling type is selected based on misalignment tolerance, torque capacity, and operating speed.

option_b: High torque capacity and axial movement capability
explanation: Answer: B | Spline connections use multiple parallel keys (teeth) machined around the shaft circumference and a matching internal spline in the hub. They provide high torque capacity distributed across multiple teeth, and depending on the fit, allow controlled axial sliding movement. Common in automotive driveshafts, gearboxes, and tool holders.

option_d: Compensates for shaft deflection and misalignment
explanation: Answer: D | Self-aligning ball bearings have a spherically curved outer raceway that allows the inner ring and ball assembly to tilt up to 2-3 degrees relative to the outer ring. This compensates for shaft deflection under load, housing bore misalignment, and mounting errors. They are used on long shafts prone to deflection, conveyor pulleys, and agricultural equipment.

question_text: Duty cycle on a welder indicates:
option_a: The percentage of 10 minutes it can operate at rated output
option_b: How long it can weld continuously
explanation: Answer: A | Duty cycle is the percentage of a 10-minute period that a welder can operate at its rated output current without overheating. Example: a 200A welder with 60% duty cycle can weld for 6 minutes out of every 10 minutes at 200A. Exceeding the duty cycle activates the thermal protection circuit. Duty cycle increases at lower amperage settings.

Answer: B | Flooded suction means the pump centerline is below the liquid surface in the suction vessel, so liquid flows to the pump by gravity. This maximizes the available NPSH (NPSHa), eliminates the need for priming, and ensures the pump remains primed. It is the preferred installation arrangement for centrifugal pumps whenever the tank elevation permits.

option_c: Multiple belts run on same sheaves
explanation: Answer: C | Matched V-belts are required when multiple belts run in parallel on the same sheaves. Unmatched belts of the same nominal size can have small length differences, causing uneven load sharing where shorter belts carry most of the load and wear out prematurely. Matched sets are measured and coded to within tight length tolerances.

Answer: B | Carbon content is the most important factor affecting steel's hardness and strength. Low carbon steel (< 0.30% C) is soft, ductile, and weldable but cannot be hardened significantly by heat treatment. High carbon steel (0.60-1.0% C) can be hardened to high hardness but becomes more brittle and difficult to weld. Medium carbon steel (0.30-0.60% C) balances strength and toughness.

question_text: The main advantage of a synchronous belt drive over V-belts is:
explanation: Answer: B | Synchronous (timing) belts use teeth that mesh with grooved pulleys, providing a positive drive with zero slip. This makes them ideal for applications requiring precise speed ratios and timing, such as camshafts and CNC machines. V-belts rely on friction and can slip under overload or improper tension.

Answer: A | Sprockets are classified by pitch (distance between chain pin centers, e.g., 1/2 = #40 chain) and number of teeth, which together determine the sprocket's pitch diameter and speed ratio. Sprocket pitch must match chain pitch exactly. The number of teeth affects smoothness  more teeth on the driven sprocket gives smoother, quieter operation.

option_a: Allows injection of flush or seal liquid
explanation: Answer: A | A lantern ring (seal cage) is a hollow ring placed in the middle of the packing rings in the stuffing box. It has radial holes that allow injection of clean flush liquid (or lubricant) under pressure to lubricate the packing, cool the shaft sleeve, and prevent process fluid from contaminating the packing area in hazardous fluid applications.

question_text: Sheave groove wear is indicated by:
option_a: Belt riding lower in the groove
explanation: Answer: A | Worn sheave grooves allow the belt to ride lower in the groove, reducing the contact arc and effective grip. A new belt in a worn sheave will bottom out in the groove, losing the wedging action that provides traction. Check with a groove gauge; replace sheave if worn beyond specification.

question_text: If a driver gear has 20 teeth and driven gear has 40 teeth, the speed ratio is:
explanation: Answer: C | Speed ratio = driver teeth / driven teeth. With 20 driver teeth and 40 driven teeth, speed ratio = 20/40 = 1:2, meaning the driven gear runs at half the speed (speed reduction). Torque is inversely proportional: driven torque = driver torque x 2. This is a 2:1 gear reduction in torque terms.

Answer: A | Annealing involves heating steel to above its critical temperature (austenizing), then very slowly cooling it (typically in the furnace) to produce a soft, ductile structure with relieved internal stresses. Annealing improves machinability, restores ductility after cold working, and relieves residual stresses from welding or forming operations.

question_text: A back pullout pump design allows:
explanation: Answer: B | A back pullout (or back pull-out) pump design allows the rotating assembly (impeller, shaft, seal/packing, and bearing housing) to be withdrawn from the rear without disconnecting the suction and discharge piping or disturbing the pump casing. This greatly reduces maintenance downtime, especially when frequent impeller or seal replacement is needed.

option_c: Delivers constant flow regardless of pressure
explanation: Answer: C | Positive displacement pumps deliver a nearly constant flow rate per revolution or stroke regardless of system pressure (within design limits). Examples include gear pumps, piston pumps, diaphragm pumps, and vane pumps. They must have a pressure relief valve in the discharge line because they will build pressure to the point of mechanical failure if the discharge is blocked.

Answer: A | Per the centrifugal pump affinity laws, increasing impeller diameter increases both flow (Q) and head (H). Flow increases proportionally to diameter ratio; head increases as the square of the diameter ratio. Impeller trimming (reducing diameter) is commonly done to match pump performance to system requirements without changing speed.

Answer: C | Bearings must be stored in their original sealed packaging to prevent contamination and corrosion. Store horizontally (flat) to prevent the grease from migrating to one side of sealed bearings. Maintain controlled temperature (15-25°C) and relative humidity below 60%. Avoid areas with vibration, which can cause false brinelling in stored bearings. Rotate old stock first (FIFO).
